KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define "Water Extract" in tea and state its FSSAI significance.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** Water extract, also called **total soluble solids**, is the percentage of tea constituents that dissolve in boiling water during brewing. It is determined by **BIS 3633** method: 2 g of tea is boiled with 200 mL water, filtered, and the filtrate is evaporated to dryness; the residue is weighed and expressed as % of original sample. **FSSAI significance:** It indicates the **brewing strength and quality** of tea. The minimum BIS limit is **≥ 32 %** for CTC tea and **≥ 36 %** for orthodox tea. Low water extract indicates **adulteration, exhausted leaves, or over-drying**, and the lot may be rejected under FSSAI Standards 2.10.1. **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List any three adulterants commonly found in coffee powder and the rapid test to detect them.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three common adulterants in coffee powder are: > 1. **Roasted chicory** — detected by the **cold-water test** (chicory sinks and releases colour quickly, coffee floats). > 2. **Roasted barley or cereal starch** — detected by the **iodine test** (blue-black colour with iodine solution indicates starch). > 3. **Tamarind or date seed powder** — detected by the **microscopic test** and the **ash test** (excess insoluble ash > 4 %). > Rapid on-the-spot detection uses the **float test** (pure coffee floats, adulterants sink). --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer (Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts, (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Total = 14 marks. Module-level choice. #### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] Describe the determination of moisture content in tea by the hot-air oven method. Give the formula, BIS standard limit, and significance.** **Model Solution:** Moisture content is the most fundamental quality parameter for any beverage raw material. The hot-air oven method (BIS 10226 / AOAC 920.149) is the **primary reference method** validated globally. **Principle:** When a known weight of tea is heated in a hot-air oven at **100 ± 2 °C** to constant weight, the moisture and other volatile matter escape. The loss in weight is calculated as moisture and expressed as **% w/w**. **Apparatus and Reagents:** Hot-air oven (with ±1 °C accuracy), desiccator with silica gel, petri dish or moisture dish (aluminium/glass, with lid), analytical balance (0.1 mg), tongs. **Procedure:** 1. Dry the empty petri dish with lid in the oven at 100 °C for 1 hour; cool in desiccator for 30 minutes; weigh ($W_e$). 2. Accurately weigh **5 g** of well-mixed ground tea into the dish ($W_{es}$). 3. Place the dish (lid removed) in the oven at **100 ± 2 °C** for **3–4 hours**. 4. Replace the lid, cool in desiccator for 30 minutes, weigh. 5. Reheat for 1 hour, cool, weigh. Repeat until two consecutive weighings differ by **< 1 mg** (constant weight, $W_{ds}$). **Formula:** $$ \text{Moisture (\% w/w)} = \dfrac{W_{es} - W_{ds}}{W_{es} - W_e} \times 100 $$ **BIS Standard Limit:** For black tea, **moisture ≤ 10 % w/w** (BIS 3613); for green tea, **≤ 6 %** is preferred for safe storage. **Significance (3 marks):** - **Shelf life:** High moisture promotes microbial growth (Aspergillus, Penicillium moulds) producing **mycotoxins** (aflatoxin B1). - **Quality:** Affects tea aroma, brittleness, and the brewing yield. - **Economic:** Buyers pay per kg; excess moisture means the buyer pays for water. - **Storage safety:** Moisture > 12 % may cause **self-heating** in bulk silos. **Result interpretation:** A value of **7.8 %** is acceptable; **> 10 %** is **non-compliant** under FSSAI. --- **Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Lowenthal's Permanganate method for tannin estimation in tea. Write the principle, procedure, and significance.** **Model Solution:** Tannins in tea are **polyphenolic compounds** (catechin gallates) that contribute to **astringency, body, and colour** of the brewed liquor. The classical titration method is the **Lowenthal-Procter Permanganate Method**, standardized by BIS. **Principle:** Tannins (and other polyphenols) are **oxidized by potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) in the presence of indigo carmine indicator**. The disappearance of the blue colour marks the endpoint. A correction is applied by titrating the **non-tannin fraction** (the filtrate after precipitating tannins with gelatin or hide powder). The difference gives **true tannin content**. **Reagents:** 0.1 N KMnO₄, indigo carmine indicator, hide powder (or gelatin-NaCl solution), distilled water. **Procedure:** 1. Extract **5 g** of tea in 400 mL boiling water, filter, make up to 500 mL. 2. **Total polyphenols titration:** Take 10 mL extract + 25 mL indigo carmine; titrate with 0.1 N KMnO₄ until blue → green → golden yellow endpoint. Note volume $V_1$. 3. **Blank titration:** Take 25 mL indigo carmine + water; titrate with KMnO₄. Note volume $V_2$. 4. **Non-tannin titration:** Mix 10 mL extract with hide powder/gelatin to remove tannins, filter, titrate filtrate similarly. Note volume $V_3$. 5. Calculate tannin as **tannic acid equivalent** using formula: $$ \text{Tannin (\% w/w)} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2 - V_3) \times 0.004157 \times \text{Total Volume}}{W \times \text{Aliquot}} \times 100 $$ The factor **0.004157** is the tannic acid equivalent in grams per mL of 0.1 N KMnO₄. **Significance:** - Tannins give tea its **characteristic briskness**; BIS limit is **≥ 9 %** for CTC tea. - High tannin → strong astringency (acceptable in breakfast tea); low tannin → flat cup. - Tannins also contribute to **antioxidant activity**; health claim-bearing teas (green tea) have higher tannin (epigallocatechin gallate, EGCG) content. --- #### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the determination of caffeine content in coffee by UV-Vis spectrophotometry. Include the principle, extraction procedure, and calculation.** **Model Solution:** Caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine) is the principal alkaloid in coffee. The UV-Vis method (AOAC 960.25) is **rapid, cheap, and lab-friendly**, suitable for KTU-level quality analysis. **Principle:** Caffeine absorbs UV light maximally at **λ_max = 275 nm** (in chloroform) and **273 nm** (in water). The absorbance is proportional to concentration by the **Beer-Lambert Law**, $A = \varepsilon c l$. A standard calibration curve using **pure caffeine** (5, 10, 15, 20, 25 µg/mL) is plotted. **Reagents:** Chloroform (AR grade), 0.1 N HCl, NaOH, anhydrous Na₂SO₄, pure caffeine standard (Sigma/Merck). **Procedure:** 1. Accurately weigh **2.0 g** of roasted, ground coffee in a 250 mL flask. 2. Add 100 mL distilled water; boil for 10 minutes with occasional shaking. 3. Cool; add 5 mL of 1 N NaOH; transfer to a separatory funnel. 4. Extract with chloroform (3 × 25 mL); collect the organic layers. 5. Pass through anhydrous Na₂SO₄; evaporate chloroform on a water bath at 60 °C. 6. Re-dissolve the residue in 50 mL chloroform (volumetric flask) — this is the **sample extract**. 7. Read absorbance at 275 nm against chloroform blank. 8. Plot standard curve; determine concentration of sample. **Calculation:** $$ \text{Caffeine (\% w/w)} = \dfrac{C \times V \times D}{W \times 1000} \times 100 $$ Where $C$ = µg/mL from graph, $V$ = 50 mL, $D$ = dilution factor, $W$ = 2.0 g. **Typical Result:** Arabica roasted coffee: **1.0–1.5 %**; Robusta: **2.0–2.7 %**. Sample reporting: "**1.48 % caffeine on dry basis** — conforms to Arabica standard." --- **Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] Explain the detection of chicory adulteration in coffee. State the principle of three specific tests and their interpretation.** **Model Solution:** Chicory is the most common adulterant in coffee powder because it is **cheaper (1/4th price)** and provides a similar dark-brown colour and body. The FSSAI mandate: pure coffee powder must contain **0 % chicory** unless labelled "**Coffee-Chicory Mixture**" (where minimum 51 % coffee is required under Standard 2.10.2). **Test 1: Cold Water Float Test** - **Principle:** Roasted coffee has entrapped gases and hydrophobic lipids; it **floats** and releases colour slowly. Chicory is water-soluble and dense. - **Procedure:** Sprinkle 1 g of sample gently on 10 mL cold water in a beaker. - **Observation:** Pure coffee → particles **float, no brown colour in water for 1–2 min**. Adulterated → **particles sink, brown colour diffuses within seconds**. **Test 2: Iodine Test (Starch Detection)** - **Principle:** Chicory root contains **inulin** and trace starch; iodine forms a **blue-black complex** with helical polysaccharides. - **Procedure:** Boil 2 g sample in 20 mL water, cool, add 1–2 drops of **0.1 N iodine solution**. - **Observation:** Pure coffee → **no colour change** (or faint yellow). Adulterated → **blue-black colour** appears. **Test 3: Caffeine Detection (UV)** - **Principle:** Pure chicory contains **zero caffeine**; coffee has ≥ 1 %. UV absorbance at 275 nm of the chloroform extract is **proportional to caffeine**. - **Procedure:** Extract 5 g sample with hot water, defat, partition into chloroform, read A at 275 nm. - **Observation:** A < 0.05 (essentially no caffeine) → **suspected chicory**. A > 0.3 → **pure coffee**. **Bonus Test — Ash Test:** Pure coffee ash is **3.5–4.5 %**; chicory ash is **3–5 %**, but **water-soluble ash of chicory is much higher** (chicory = 70 %, coffee = 55 %). The water-soluble ash of an unknown sample > 65 % suggests chicory content. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Units error in caffeine formula:** Forgetting the factor of **1000** for µg→mg conversion (lose 2 marks). > 2. **Drying temperature confusion:** Always use **100–105 °C** for moisture. Using 110 °C may char sugars and give **erroneously high** moisture. > 3. **Sample cooling in desiccator:** Hot samples gain moisture on cooling in humid Kerala atmosphere. The **desiccator step is non-negotiable** — skipping it loses 1 mark. > 4. **Ash endpoint confusion:** "Grey-white ash" is the **correct endpoint**; "red-hot ash" means **incomplete organic destruction** and **over-firing** causes loss of alkaline carbonates → under-estimated alkalinity. > 5. **Distinguishing coffee vs. chicory in the float test:** Students often write "coffee sinks" — **WRONG, coffee FLOATS**. This single mistake cost students 2 marks in the Dec 2023 paper. > 6. **Forgetting FSSAI limits in long answers:** Always cite the **BIS/FSSAI limit value** along with the test result. The examiner awards **1.5 marks** for compliance interpretation. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Moisture** is determined by the **hot-air oven method at 100 ± 2 °C**; FSSAI limit for tea ≤ 10 %, coffee ≤ 5 %, cocoa powder ≤ 7 %, chicory ≤ 12 %. - ✅ **Total ash** is the residue after **muffle furnace ignition at 550 ± 10 °C**; pure tea ash = 4–8 %; coffee ash = 3.5–4.5 %; chicory ash = 3.5–6.0 %. - ✅ **Water-soluble ash** must be **≥ 40 %** of total ash for tea; **≥ 60 %** of total ash for coffee. - ✅ **Alkalinity of ash** formula uses the **equivalent weight of K₂O = 47** (or 0.047 when using mL of 0.1 N acid). - ✅ **Caffeine** is estimated by **UV-Vis at 275 nm** in chloroform; typical ranges: tea 1.0–3.0 %, coffee Arabica 1.0–1.5 %, Robusta 2.0–2.7 %, cocoa 0.1–0.7 %, chicory = 0 %. - ✅ **Tannin** is estimated by **Lowenthal's permanganate** or **Folin-Ciocalteu** method; tea should have ≥ 9 % tannin. - ✅ **Water extract (Total Soluble Solids)** indicates brewing strength; minimum **32 %** for CTC tea, **65 %** for chicory. - ✅ **Coffee adulteration tests:** **Cold water float test** (coffee floats, chicory sinks), **iodine test** (chicory/starch = blue-black), **UV caffeine test** (chicory = zero caffeine). - ✅ **Sensory evaluation** follows the **SCA / Q-Grader cupping protocol** with 9 attributes scored on 100-point scale; specialty grade requires **> 80**. - ✅ **Lab report must include:** sample ID, BIS method number, calculation with units, FSSAI limit, pass/fail conclusion, and CO/PO mapping. - ✅ **Chicory** is rich in **inulin** (15–20 %) and is used as a **prebiotic coffee extender**; pure chicory extract shows a **deep brown colour** with **no caffeine**. - ✅ **Cocoa alkaloid** is mainly **theobromine** (0.5–2.7 %); caffeine is minor (0.1–0.7 %). - ✅ **FSSAI Regulation Reference:** Standards **2.10.1 (Tea), 2.10.2 (Coffee), 2.10.4 (Chicory), 2.10.5 (Cocoa/Chocolate)**. - ✅ **Total ash values to memorize:** Tea 4–8 % | Coffee 3.5–4.5 % | Cocoa 6–8 % | Chicory 3.5–6 %. - ✅ **Alkalinity factor in titration:** Always use **0.047 g (milli-equivalent of K₂O)** for tea ash; equivalent weight of K₂O = 47. ---

topics.title: Quality Analysis of Sugar and Confectionery Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL407 - FOOD ANALYSIS AND QUALITY EVALUATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Analysis of Sugar and Confectionery Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Analysis of Sugar and Confectionery Products** refers to the systematic application of physico-chemical, optical, and sensory methods to evaluate the purity, compositional integrity, and acceptability of refined sugars, raw sugars, and sugar-based confectionery items (hard-boiled candies, toffees, fondants, marshmallows, chocolates, gums, jellies, etc.) against established national and international standards such as **BIS (IS 498:2018)**, **ICUMSA**, **FSSAI**, and **Codex Alimentarius**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Quality analysis of sugar and confectionery involves determination of polarization, ICUMSA colour, reducing sugars, moisture, ash, sulphated ash, brix, acidity, hardness, and sensory parameters using polarimetry, refractometry, titration, and gravimetric methods."*

The two major product groups analyzed are:

1. **Sugar Products** – Granulated white sugar (ICUMSA 45), refined sugar, raw sugar, jaggery, khandsari, liquid glucose, invert syrup, and high-fructose corn syrup.
2. **Confectionery Products** – Hard candies, toffees/caramels, fondants, marshmallows, chewing gums, lozenges, panned goods, chocolate-coated products, and gummy/jelly candies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sugar Doctor's Health Check-up"**
> 
> Think of sugar and confectionery quality analysis exactly like a routine medical check-up at a doctor's clinic:
> 
> - **Polarization (Polarimeter)** = the **eye test** → measures how pure the crystalline sugar is (sucrose content).
> - **Refractometry (Brix)** = the **BMI (body-mass index)** → measures how much dissolved sugar sits in a syrup.
> - **Moisture (Oven Drying)** = checking for **dehydration** → too much water spoils the candy, too little makes it brittle.
> - **Colour (ICUMSA Units)** = the **skin complexion test** → a whiter, cleaner sugar scores higher.
> - **Reducing Sugar (Lane-Eynon titration)** = the **blood-sugar test** → too much invert sugar means the product has degraded and will absorb moisture.
> - **Sensory Evaluation** = the patient **self-reporting** → does it taste right, feel right, look right?
> 
> Just as a doctor combines several tests to issue a final health certificate, a food analyst combines physical, chemical, and sensory tests to certify the product's quality.

### 1.3 Key Standards & Reference Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Quality Benchmarks for Refined White Sugar (BIS IS 498:2018 / ICUMSA):**
> 
> - **Polarization (Sucrose %)** ≥ **99.5%** by weight (Polarimetric, ICUMSA GS2/3-1).
> - **ICUMSA Colour Units** ≤ **80 IU** at 420 nm for refined sugar.
> - **Invert Sugar (Reducing Sugars)** ≤ **0.1%** by weight.
> - **Moisture (Loss on Drying)** ≤ **0.1%** (vacuum oven, 105 °C, 3 h).
> - **Ash (Conductivity / Sulphated)** ≤ **0.1%** by weight.
> - **Insoluble Matter** ≤ **0.04%**.
> - **SO₂ (Sulphur Dioxide)** ≤ **70 mg/kg** (refined) / **20 mg/kg** (plantation white).

### 1.4 Classification of Sugar Quality by ICUMSA Colour

| Grade | ICUMSA Units (IU at 420 nm) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| ICUMSA 45 | **≤ 45** | Premium refined white sugar (export grade) |
| ICUMSA 100 | **≤ 100** | Standard refined white sugar (retail) |
| ICUMSA 150 | **≤ 150** | Pharmaceutical-grade |
| ICUMSA 600–1200 | **600 – 1200** | Raw / VHP (Very High Polarization) sugar |
| ICUMSA 1500+ | **> 1500** | Brown / Khandsari / Jaggery |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Overview of Analytical Parameters Tested

Sugar and confectionery products are evaluated on **three pillars** of analysis:

1. **Purity Parameters** – Polarization, Brix, specific rotation.
2. **Safety & Stability Parameters** – Moisture, ash, SO₂, pH, acidity, water activity (a_w).
3. **Sensory & Functional Parameters** – Colour (ICUMSA), hardness (penetrometer), texture, flavour, and microbial limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both physical AND chemical tests?**
> A sugar may be chemically pure (high polarization) but physically poor (yellow colour, high ash). Likewise, a candy may pass moisture test but fail on hardness. Hence the KTU lab mandates a **multi-parameter** analysis to mimic real industry QC (Quality Control) protocols.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Test

**A. Polarimetric Determination of Sucrose (Polarimeter / Saccharimeter)**

- **Why?** Sucrose is **optically active** — it rotates plane-polarized light to the right (dextrorotatory). The angle of rotation is proportional to concentration.
- **How?** A solution of known concentration (Brix-corrected to **26 g/100 mL**) is read on a saccharimeter. The result in **°Z (Zucker degree)** directly gives the International Sugar Scale percentage.

**B. Brix / Total Soluble Solids (TSS) by Refractometer**

- **Why?** Brix represents the % w/w of dissolved solids (mostly sugars) in syrups and confectionery. It governs **texture, shelf life, and sweetness**.
- **How?** Refractometer measures refractive index at 20 °C; automatic temperature compensation is applied.

**C. Moisture (Loss on Drying – LOD)**

- **Why?** Moisture drives **caking, microbial growth, and stickiness** in candy. Standard for granulated sugar: ≤ 0.1%; for hard candy: ≤ 3%; for toffee: 4 – 8%.
- **How?** Vacuum oven at **70 °C, 3 h** (sugar) or **105 °C, 2 h** (confectionery), desiccator cooling, gravimetric difference.

**D. ICUMSA Colour at 420 nm**

- **Why?** Yellow tinting in sugar comes from **caramelized phenolics and melanoidins** formed during processing.
- **How?** Sugar solution filtered through 0.45 µm membrane; absorbance read at **420 nm** in a 1 cm cell. ICUMSA Units = Absorbance × 1000 / (Brix × Path length).

**E. Reducing Sugars (Lane–Eynon / DNS / Titrimetric)**

- **Why?** Invert sugar (glucose + fructose) from sucrose hydrolysis is hygroscopic — high RS causes **caking, colour darkening, and storage loss**.
- **How?** Fehling's A + B titration, methylene blue indicator; result expressed as % invert on dry basis.

**F. Ash (Sulphated / Conductimetric)**

- **Why?** Ash = total inorganic residue; indicator of **mineral contamination and refining efficiency**.
- **How?** Muffle furnace at **550 °C** (sulphated ash, ICUMSA GS2/3-7) or conductivity-based ash (ICUMSA GS2/3-4).

**G. Confectionery-Specific Parameters**

- **Hardness** – Penetrometer / Texture Analyzer (TA-XT2), expressed in **Newtons (N)** or **mm penetration**.
- **Water Activity (a_w)** – Dew-point hygrometer (Aqualab); confectionery a_w typically **0.4 – 0.75**.
- **Acidity (% citric)** – Titration with **0.1 N NaOH**, phenolphthalein indicator.
- **pH** – Digital pH meter in 10 % w/v solution.
- **Sulphated Ash** – For salt-bearing confections (gums).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Method | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Polarization (Sucrose)** | $S = \dfrac{26 \times \text{Saccharimeter Reading}}{26}$ → directly read in °Z (100 °Z = 100 % sucrose at 26 g/100 mL) | % | Saccharimeter / Polarimeter | ICUMSA GS2/3-1 |
| 2 | **Specific Rotation** | $[\alpha]^{20}_D = \dfrac{\alpha}{l \times c}$ where $\alpha$ = observed rotation, $l$ = path length (dm), $c$ = concentration (g/mL) | deg·mL·g⁻¹·dm⁻¹ | Polarimeter | — |
| 3 | **Brix (TSS)** | $°Brix = n_{D}^{20} - \text{(RI-to-Brix conversion from tables)}$ | % w/w | Refractometer | ICUMSA GS4-13 |
| 4 | **ICUMSA Colour** | $ICUMSA = \dfrac{A_{420} \times 1000}{b \times c}$ where $b$ = cell path (cm), $c$ = concentration (g/mL) | IU | Spectrophotometer | ICUMSA GS2/3-9 |
| 5 | **Moisture (LOD)** | $\%\text{ Moisture} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$ | % w/w | Gravimetric | ICUMSA GS2/3-15 |
| 6 | **Reducing Sugars (Lane–Eynon)** | $\%\text{ RS} = \dfrac{F \times V \times \text{dilution}}{W} \times 100$ | % invert | Fehling titration | ICUMSA GS2/3-5 |
| 7 | **Ash Content** | $\%\text{ Ash} = \dfrac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w | Gravimetric / Conductivity | ICUMSA GS2/3-7 |
| 8 | **Water Activity** | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{sample}}{P_{water}} = \dfrac{\text{RH}\%}{100}$ | unitless | Hygrometer | — |
| 9 | **Acidity** | $\%\text{ Acidity} = \dfrac{V_{NaOH} \times N \times \text{Equivalent Weight}}{W \times 1000} \times 100$ | % | Titration | — |
| 10 | **Hardness (Penetration)** | $H = \dfrac{F}{A}$ or expressed as **mm depth** under 100 g, 5 s | N or mm | Penetrometer / Texture Analyzer | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Break Markdown Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is forbidden inside KTU table cells. Use LaTeX delimiters `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., write $a_w = p_{sample} \vert p_{water}$ if ever required) to avoid breaking table rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry & Engineering Relevance:**
> 
> - **Sugar Refineries** (EID Parry, Dhampur, Balrampur Chini Mills) operate 24 × 7 QC labs performing the exact parameters above on every batch.
> - **Confectionery Industry** (Parle, Cadbury, ITC, Mondelez) uses **Brix, water activity, and texture analysis** to standardize hard candy, toffee, and chocolate lines.
> - **Export Houses** demand ICUMSA 45 certification for shipping to EU, Middle East, and South-East Asia.
> - **FSSAI Compliance** officers test retail sugar and confectionery at port-entry points using identical protocols.
> - This experiment trains the KTU student as a **Plant QC Chemist** with full operational readiness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Polarization – Conversion to Sucrose %

The saccharimeter is calibrated such that **100 °Z = 100 % sucrose** for a 26 g sample dissolved to 100 mL read in a **200 mm tube** at **20 °C**. The general expression:

$$
S = \dfrac{\theta_{obs}}{l \times c \times [\alpha]^{20}_D \times 2.8885}
$$

However, in industry this is simplified by direct saccharimeter reading.

**Worked Example:**

A **26.00 g** sugar sample is dissolved and made to **100 mL**. The saccharimeter reads **99.2 °Z**.

> **Step 1**: Note that **99.2 °Z** directly corresponds to **99.2 % sucrose** at 26 g/100 mL, 200 mm tube, 20 °C.

> **Step 2**: Correct for instrument quartz plate calibration $C = 99.85$:
> 
> $$
> S_{corrected} = 99.2 \times \dfrac{100}{99.85}
> $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{corrected} &= 99.2 \times 1.001503 \\
&= 99.349 \\
&\approx 99.35\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3**: Apply Clerget temperature correction if read at T ≠ 20 °C (T measured in °C):
> 
> $$
> S_{20} = S_T \times \left[1 + 0.0003 \times (T - 20)\right]
> $$

Assume the reading was taken at **23 °C**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{20} &= 99.35 \times \left[1 + 0.0003 \times (23 - 20)\right] \\
&= 99.35 \times (1 + 0.0009) \\
&= 99.35 \times 1.0009 \\
&= 99.439 \\
&\approx 99.44\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4**: Apply moisture correction to report on **dry basis** if required.

**Final Answer:** **Sucrose % = 99.44 %** → Sample passes the BIS IS 498:2018 limit of ≥ 99.5 %? **MARGINAL / FAIL** → Repeat with improved refining or reject.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: ICUMSA Colour – Working Calculation

**Problem:** A 50 °Brix sugar solution is prepared at **1 g/mL** in a 1 cm path length cell. The absorbance at 420 nm is **0.045**. Calculate ICUMSA Units.

**Formula:**

$$
ICUMSA\ (IU) = \dfrac{A_{420} \times 1000}{b \times c}
$$

Where:
- $A_{420} = 0.045$
- $b = 1\ cm$
- $c = 1\ g/mL$ (concentration of solution)

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ICUMSA &= \dfrac{0.045 \times 1000}{1 \times 1} \\
&= 45\ IU
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 45 IU classifies the sample as **ICUMSA 45 grade (premium refined white sugar)** suitable for export. ✅

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Moisture by Loss on Drying (LOD)

**Problem:** A **5.000 g** sugar sample is dried in a vacuum oven. Crucible empty weight **W₀ = 28.4520 g**. Crucible + sample before drying **W₁ = 33.4520 g**. Crucible + sample after drying **W₂ = 33.4470 g**. Calculate % moisture.

**Formula:**

$$
\%\text{ Moisture} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step:**

> **Step 1:** Compute **W₁ − W₀** (sample mass):
> 
> $$
> W_1 - W_0 = 33.4520 - 28.4520 = 5.0000\ g
> $$

> **Step 2:** Compute **W₁ − W₂** (moisture mass):
> 
> $$
> W_1 - W_2 = 33.4520 - 33.4470 = 0.0050\ g
> $$

> **Step 3:** Substitute:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \%\text{ Moisture} &= \dfrac{0.0050}{5.0000} \times 100 \\
> &= 0.10\ \%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Interpretation:** Moisture = 0.10 % → equals BIS limit of 0.1 % → **PASS (Borderline)**. ✅

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Reducing Sugars by Lane–Eynon Titration

**Problem:** Fehling's Factor F = 1.020 mg invert per 10 mL Fehling's. Sample weight **W = 5.00 g**, diluted to **250 mL**; **25 mL** aliquot titrated; titre **V = 19.50 mL**. Calculate % reducing sugars.

**Formula:**

$$
\%\text{ RS} = \dfrac{F \times V \times 250}{W \times 25} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step:**

> **Step 1:** Compute the invert sugar in the 25 mL titre:
> 
> $$
> \text{Invert per titre} = F \times V = 1.020 \times 19.50 = 19.89\ mg
> $$

> **Step 2:** Scale to full 250 mL:
> 
> $$
> \text{Total invert in 250 mL} = 19.89 \times \dfrac{250}{25} = 198.9\ mg = 0.1989\ g
> $$

> **Step 3:** Express as % of sample:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \%\text{ RS} &= \dfrac{0.1989}{5.00} \times 100 \\
> &= 3.978\ \% \\
&\approx 3.98\ \%
\end{aligned}
> $$

> **Interpretation:** Reducing sugars = 3.98 % → For a hard candy (specification 1.5 – 4 %), this is **PASS**. For refined white sugar (specification ≤ 0.1 %), this would be a **FAIL** indicating severe inversion during storage.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Hardness by Penetrometer (Confectionery)

**Problem:** A hard candy is tested with a **cone penetrometer** under **100 g load for 5 seconds**. Penetration depth = **3.2 mm**. Calculate hardness index and interpret.

**Formula:**

$$
Hardness\ Index\ (N) = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{100 \times 9.81 \times t}{d^2 \times \pi / 4}
$$

Where $F$ = force (N), $A$ = cone contact area, $d$ = penetration depth.

**Step-by-step:**

> **Step 1:** Force:
> 
> $$
> F = 100\ g \times 9.81\ m/s^2 = 0.981\ N
> $$

> **Step 2:** Assuming a standard cone of **6 mm base diameter** $d_c = 6\ mm$:
> 
> $$
> A = \dfrac{\pi \times (6)^2}{4} = 28.27\ mm^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2
> $$

> **Step 3:** Hardness Index:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> H &= \dfrac{0.981}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} \\
> &= 34{,}696\ Pa \\
> &= 34.7\ kPa
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Interpretation:** Penetration depth **3.2 mm** indicates a **firm, well-bodied hard candy** (spec: 1 – 4 mm). Hardness index 34.7 kPa is within the standard hard candy range (**25 – 60 kPa**). ✅

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Acidity of a Confectionery Product

**Problem:** **10.00 g** of toffee dissolved in **100 mL** distilled water. Titrated against **0.1 N NaOH**, titre = **4.20 mL**. Phenolphthalein end-point. Calculate % acidity (expressed as **citric acid**, eq. wt. = 64).

**Formula:**

$$
\%\text{ Acidity} = \dfrac{V \times N \times E}{W \times 1000} \times 100
$$

**Step-by-step:**

> **Step 1:** Substitute:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \%\text{ Acidity} &= \dfrac{4.20 \times 0.1 \times 64}{10.00 \times 1000} \times 100 \\
> &= \dfrac{26.88}{10000} \times 100 \\
> &= 0.269\ \%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Interpretation:** Toffee acidity = 0.27 % (as citric acid) → falls within typical **0.1 – 0.5 %** for fruit-flavored toffees. ✅

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix – Cross-Reference Table (Sugars vs. Confectionery)

| Parameter | White Sugar Spec | Brown Sugar Spec | Hard Candy Spec | Toffee Spec |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polarization / Brix | ≥ 99.5 % / — | ≥ 96 % / — | 75 – 85 °Brix | 80 – 88 °Brix |
| Moisture | ≤ 0.1 % | ≤ 1.5 % | ≤ 3 % | 4 – 8 % |
| ICUMSA Colour | ≤ 80 IU | 600 – 1500 IU | — | — |
| Reducing Sugars | ≤ 0.1 % | 1 – 5 % | 1.5 – 4 % | 5 – 15 % |
| Ash | ≤ 0.1 % | 0.5 – 1.5 % | ≤ 0.5 % | ≤ 0.8 % |
| Acidity (citric) | — | — | 0.1 – 0.3 % | 0.1 – 0.5 % |
| Water Activity | 0.30 – 0.50 | 0.40 – 0.60 | 0.40 – 0.60 | 0.55 – 0.75 |
| Hardness (penetration) | — | — | 1 – 4 mm | 4 – 8 mm |
| SO₂ | ≤ 70 mg/kg | — | ≤ 50 mg/kg | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sugar & Confectionery QC Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Material Receipt] --> A1[Sample Login and Labelling]
    A1 --> A2[Sample Homogenization and Quartering]
    A2 --> B0{Choose Analytical Track}
    B0 -- Liquid Syrup Track --> B1[Brix by Refractometer]
    B0 -- Solid Sugar Track --> B2[Polarization by Saccharimeter]
    B1 --> C1[Polarization Optional for Syrup]
    B2 --> C2[ICUMSA Colour at 420 nm]
    C1 --> D1[Acidity Titration with NaOH]
    C2 --> D2[Reducing Sugar Lane Eynon]
    D1 --> E1[Moisture by Vacuum Oven]
    D2 --> E2[Ash by Muffle Furnace]
    E1 --> F0{Confectionery Or Bulk Sugar}
    E2 --> F0
    F0 -- Confectionery --> G1[Water Activity Test]
    F0 -- Bulk Sugar --> G2[Particle Size and Sieve Analysis]
    G1 --> H1[Hardness Test by Penetrometer]
    G2 --> H2[Insoluble Matter Test]
    H1 --> I1[Sensory Evaluation Panel]
    H2 --> I2[Sensory Evaluation Panel]
    I1 --> J1[Compilation of QC Report]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1{Acceptance Decision}
    K1 -- Pass --> L1[Certify Batch and Release]
    K1 -- Fail --> L2[Reject Batch and Trigger CAPA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** This mermaid block represents a complete KTU 2024 Lab workflow — from raw material receipt to the final Quality Control (QC) decision — modelled on a typical Indian sugar refinery + confectionery plant (e.g., Parle-G or EID Parry QC lab).

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Instrument-to-Parameter Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sugar Sample in 100 mL Volumetric Flask] --> S2[Saccharimeter 200 mm Tube]
    S2 --> S3[Polarization Result in Z]
    T1[Confectionery Sample Crushed] --> T2[Refractometer Prism]
    T2 --> T3[Brix Reading in percent]
    U1[Filter through 0.45 Micron] --> U2[Spectrophotometer 420 nm]
    U2 --> U3[ICUMSA Units IU]
    V1[Take 5 g in Crucible] --> V2[Vacuum Oven 70 deg C 3 h]
    V2 --> V3[Weigh to Constant Mass]
    V3 --> V4[Moisture percent]
    W1[Take 5 g in 250 mL Flask] --> W2[Fehling A and B 10 mL each]
    W2 --> W3[Boil and Titrate with Methylene Blue]
    W3 --> W4[Reducing Sugar percent]
    X1[Toffee 10 g in Beaker] --> X2[Add Distilled Water 100 mL]
    X2 --> X3[Add 2 drops Phenolphthalein]
    X3 --> X4[Titrate with 0.1 N NaOH]
    X4 --> X5[Acidity as Citric Acid percent]
    Y1[Hard Candy Placed on Platform] --> Y2[Cone Penetrometer with 100 g Load]
    Y2 --> Y3[Penetration Depth in mm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** Each block in the diagram corresponds to a single instrument station the KTU student will operate in the PCFTL407 lab — saccharimeter, refractometer, spectrophotometer, vacuum oven, muffle furnace, burette assembly, and penetrometer.

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: Accept / Reject Criteria (Refined White Sugar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Batch Sample Entered] --> R1{Polarization greater than 99.5 percent}
    R1 -- No --> R2[REJECT Refining Incomplete]
    R1 -- Yes --> R3{ICUMSA less than 80 IU}
    R3 -- No --> R4[REJECT Colour Above Limit]
    R3 -- Yes --> R5{Moisture less than 0.1 percent}
    R5 -- No --> R6[REJECT Caking Risk]
    R5 -- Yes --> R7{Reducing Sugars less than 0.1 percent}
    R7 -- No --> R8[REJECT Storage Inversion]
    R7 -- Yes --> R9{Ash less than 0.1 percent}
    R9 -- No --> R10[REJECT Mineral Contamination]
    R9 -- Yes --> R11[APPROVED AND RELEASED]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** During the KTU 2024 lab exam, examiners expect students to **interpret the analytical output and write a clear "ACCEPT" or "REJECT" verdict** with reasons — exactly as a plant QC chemist would. The decision matrix above is the recommended framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define ICUMSA Colour Units. State the permissible limit for refined white sugar and explain its significance in sugar quality evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- ICUMSA Colour Units (IU) = a measure of the yellowish tint of sugar measured spectrophotometrically at **420 nm** on a 1 cm cell, calculated as:
  $$ICUMSA\ (IU) = \dfrac{A_{420} \times 1000}{b \times c}$$
  where $b$ = cell path length (cm) and $c$ = concentration (g/mL).
- **Permissible limit** for refined white sugar (BIS IS 498:2018) is **≤ 80 IU**; export-grade ICUMSA 45 is **≤ 45 IU**.
- **Significance:** Indicates refining efficiency; lower IU → whiter, more refined, more consumer-acceptable sugar with fewer colour precursors (melanoidins, caramelized phenolics) that degrade storage stability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any three quality parameters tested for confectionery products and mention the instrument / method used for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Brix (Total Soluble Solids)** – **Refractometer** at 20 °C; spec 75 – 88 °Brix for candies and toffees.
2. **Water Activity (a_w)** – **Dew-point hygrometer** (Aqualab); spec 0.40 – 0.75 for safe shelf life.
3. **Hardness** – **Cone penetrometer / Texture Analyzer**; spec 1 – 4 mm penetration under 100 g, 5 s load for hard candies.
4. (Bonus) **Moisture** – **Vacuum oven / LOD** at 70 °C, 3 h (sugar) or 105 °C, 2 h (confectionery).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the determination of **sucrose by polarimetry**. With a worked example, show how the polarization of a 26 g/100 mL sugar solution reading **99.20 °Z** on a saccharimeter is corrected for instrument quartz calibration (C = 99.85) and temperature (read at 24 °C), and interpret the result against BIS IS 498:2018.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Sucrose is optically active and rotates plane-polarized monochromatic sodium D-light (589.3 nm) to the right. The rotation is proportional to concentration and is read on a saccharimeter pre-calibrated in °Z (International Sugar Scale).

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **26.00 g ± 0.02 g** of the sugar sample.
2. Transfer to a 100 mL volumetric flask; dissolve in distilled water; add **basic lead acetate** (clarifying agent) if coloured, then neutralize with oxalic acid; make to mark; filter.
3. Transfer to a **200 mm polarimeter tube**.
4. Place in the saccharimeter; record the reading in **°Z**.
5. Multiply by **100/100** to obtain % sucrose on the international scale (26 g/100 mL basis).

**Worked Example (Correction Steps):**

> **Step 1:** Read observed value $= 99.20\ °Z$.

> **Step 2:** Apply quartz plate calibration $C = 99.85$:
> 
> $$
> S_{cal} = 99.20 \times \dfrac{100}{99.85} = 99.349\ °Z
> $$

> **Step 3:** Temperature correction (read at 24 °C, 0.0003 factor/°C):
> 
> $$
> S_{20} = 99.349 \times \left[1 + 0.0003 \times (24 - 20)\right] = 99.349 \times 1.0012 = 99.468\ °Z
> $$

> **Step 4:** Interpret against BIS IS 498:2018:
> - **Required limit** = ≥ 99.5 % sucrose.
> - **Obtained** = **99.47 %** → **MARGINAL FAIL** (0.03 % below limit).
> - **Action:** Re-test in triplicate, improve clarification, or declare batch sub-grade (e.g., as S-30 grade).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Lane–Eynon titrimetric method** for the determination of **reducing sugars in confectionery**. A **5.0 g** sample dissolved in **500 mL** gives a titre of **22.0 mL** against Fehling's mixture (Factor = 1.020 mg invert per 10 mL Fehling's). Calculate % reducing sugars and interpret.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Reducing sugars (glucose, fructose, maltose) reduce **Cu²⁺ (blue)** in alkaline Fehling's solution to **Cu₂O (red precipitate)**. Methylene blue indicator marks the end-point. Volume of sample solution required to completely reduce the Fehling's mixture is inversely proportional to RS concentration.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **5.0 g** sample, dissolve, clarify with lead acetate, delead with oxalate, dilute to 500 mL.
2. Take **10 mL Fehling's A + 10 mL Fehling's B** in a conical flask.
3. Heat to boil; titrate with sample solution; near end-point add **2 – 3 drops methylene blue**; complete titration within **3 minutes total boiling**.
4. Record titre **V = 22.0 mL** (matched against standard for Fehling's factor).

**Calculation:**

> **Step 1:** Fehling's factor $F = 1.020$ mg invert per 10 mL Fehling's (i.e., 20 mL combined).
> 
> **Step 2:** Standard Lane–Eynon formula:
> 
> $$
> \%\text{ RS (as invert)} = \dfrac{F \times V \times D}{W \times 1000} \times 100
> $$
> 
> where $D$ = dilution factor = $500/5 = 100$, $W = 5.0$ g, $V = 22.0$ mL.

> **Step 3:** Substitute:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \%\text{ RS} &= \dfrac{1.020 \times 22.0 \times 100}{5.0 \times 1000} \times 100 \\
> &= \dfrac{2244}{5000} \times 100 \\
> &= 44.88\ \%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 4:** Interpretation: A reducing sugar content of 44.88 % is **excessively high** for any confectionery specification (spec for hard candy 1.5 – 4 %, toffee 5 – 15 %). This indicates **severe inversion** — the sucrose has hydrolyzed during processing or storage. **REJECT** the batch.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and procedure for determining **Moisture by Loss on Drying (LOD)** for (i) refined sugar and (ii) hard candy. Give the BIS / FSSAI specification for each and the practical consequences of exceeding it.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Moisture is the mass of water lost from a sample when heated under specified conditions (vacuum oven for sugar to avoid caramelization; convection oven for confectionery). Determined gravimetrically.

**Procedure (Refined Sugar):**
1. Pre-dry silica gel in a desiccator; pre-heat vacuum oven to **70 °C**.
2. Weigh clean dry crucible (with lid) $W_0$.
3. Add **5.0 g** of sugar, weigh $W_1$.
4. Place in vacuum oven at **70 °C, 25 mm Hg, 3 hours**.
5. Cool in desiccator 30 min; weigh $W_2$. Repeat to constant mass.

**Procedure (Hard Candy):**
1. Crush the candy in a mortar.
2. Weigh **5.0 g** in a pre-weighed moisture dish with sand.
3. Dry in a hot-air oven at **105 °C, 2 hours**.
4. Cool in desiccator; weigh; repeat to constant mass.

**Specifications:**

| Product | Specification | Method | Consequence of Exceeding |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refined sugar | ≤ 0.1 % | Vacuum oven, 70 °C, 3 h | Caking, microbial spoilage, weight loss disputes |
| Hard candy | ≤ 3 % | Convection oven, 105 °C, 2 h | Stickiness, surface sweating, short shelf life |

**Formula:** $\%\text{ Moisture} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the determination of **ICUMSA colour** for refined sugar. A sugar solution is prepared at **50 °Brix and 1 g/mL**; the absorbance at 420 nm (1 cm cell) is **0.062**. Calculate the ICUMSA value and grade the sample.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** The yellow-brown tint of sugar is due to trace phenolic and melanoidin compounds. Absorbance at **420 nm** (the wavelength of yellow absorption maximum) is measured and converted to ICUMSA Units.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare a 50 % w/w sugar solution (50 g + 50 mL water, clarified through 0.45 µm filter).
2. Zero the spectrophotometer at 420 nm with distilled water.
3. Measure absorbance in a 1 cm quartz cell.
4. Calculate ICUMSA using the standard formula.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ICUMSA &= \dfrac{A_{420} \times 1000}{b \times c} \\
&= \dfrac{0.062 \times 1000}{1 \times 1} \\
&= 62\ IU
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:**
> - **62 IU** lies between ICUMSA 45 (≤ 45) and ICUMSA 100 (≤ 100).
> - The sample qualifies as **ICUMSA 100 grade** (standard refined white sugar, BIS IS 498:2018: ≤ 80 IU).
> - Sample **PASSES** the BIS limit but is **NOT export-grade ICUMSA 45**; suitable for domestic retail sale.
> - **Recommendation:** Improve decolorization (e.g., increase carbon dosage or use ion-exchange polishing) to push colour to ≤ 45 IU for export.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Errors in ICUMSA:** Students frequently forget to multiply absorbance by 1000, producing answers 1000-fold too small. Always write: $\text{IU} = A \times 1000 / (b \times c)$.
> 2. **Wrong Cu²⁺ Reference in Lane–Eynon:** Some students use Fehling's A volume (10 mL) instead of **combined A + B (20 mL)** for Fehling's factor lookup. The factor is calibrated against combined Fehling's, not just A.
> 3. **Moisture – Dry vs Wet Basis:** Failing to state "on wet basis" or "on dry basis" loses 0.5 – 1 mark. For confectionery, LOD is reported on **wet basis**; for shelf-life, dry basis is more useful.
> 4. **No Temperature Compensation in Brix:** A Brix reading without ATC (Automatic Temperature Compensation) or manual correction at 20 °C is worth only half marks. Mention "corrected to 20 °C" explicitly.
> 5. **Polarimeter Tube Length:** Not stating **200 mm** (standard tube) in the procedure loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Sensory Evaluation Skipped:** KTU 2024 syllabus lists "sensory evaluation" as a co-equal parameter. Not mentioning colour, aroma, taste, texture, mouthfeel as a sub-head in the conclusion is a common deduction.
> 7. **Decoding Errors in Clerget Correction:** Writing $0.0003 \times (T - 20)$ but applying wrong sign (e.g., using $T - 20 = +4$ when T = 16 °C) leads to errors. **Always subtract the reference (20 °C) from measured T.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — KTU PCFTL407 — Module Experiment 2**
> 
> - **Definition:** Quality Analysis of Sugar & Confectionery = combined polarimetric, refractometric, gravimetric, titrimetric, and sensory evaluation against **BIS IS 498:2018**, **ICUMSA**, and **FSSAI**.
> - **Key Polarization Standard:** 26 g/100 mL, 200 mm tube, 20 °C, 99.5 % sucrose minimum.
> - **Quartz Plate Calibration Correction:** $S_{cal} = S_{obs} \times (100 / C)$.
> - **Clerget Temperature Correction:** $S_{20} = S_T \times [1 + 0.0003(T - 20)]$.
> - **Brix vs Polarization:** Brix = all dissolved solids (°Brix = % w/w); Polarization = sucrose-specific (optical rotation).
> - **ICUMSA Formula (must memorize):** $\text{IU} = A_{420} \times 1000 / (b \times c)$.
> - **ICUMSA 45 / 100 / 150 / 600+:** the lower the IU, the whiter the sugar; export requires ≤ 45 IU.
> - **Moisture Limits:** Sugar ≤ 0.1 %; Hard candy ≤ 3 %; Toffee 4 – 8 %; Chocolate ≤ 1 %.
> - **Reducing Sugar Method:** Lane–Eynon (Fehling + methylene blue) OR DNS (spectrophotometric) OR Bertrand.
> - **Water Activity (a_w):** Confectionery a_w 0.40 – 0.75; microbial growth suppressed below 0.60.
> - **Hardness / Penetration:** Cone penetrometer, 100 g / 5 s; spec 1 – 4 mm (hard candy), 4 – 8 mm (toffee).
> - **Ash:** Sulphated ash ≤ 0.1 % for refined sugar; conductivity-ash also accepted.
> - **Acidity:** Titration with 0.1 N NaOH, phenolphthalein; expressed as % citric acid (eq. wt. 64).
> - **Sensory 5-Point Test:** Colour, aroma, taste, texture, mouthfeel — KTU 2024 expects a short sensory note alongside analytical results.
> - **Standard Reference:** BIS IS 498:2018 (refined white sugar); FSSAI Regulations, 2011 (confectionery).
> - **Inversion Warning:** High RS in sugar = hydrolyzed sucrose = hygroscopic = storage failure.
> - **Most Common Exam Question Style:** A worked numerical on either polarization (with temperature correction), ICUMSA, moisture, or Lane–Eynon titration. Memorize the **four formulas** (Polarization, ICUMSA, Moisture, Lane–Eynon) — they cover 80 % of KTU marks.
> - **Final Verdict Format:** Always conclude with **"PASS / FAIL against the relevant BIS / FSSAI standard and state the limitation triggered."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Analysis of Fruit and Vegetable Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL407 - FOOD ANALYSIS AND QUALITY EVALUATION LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Analysis of Fruit and Vegetable Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Analysis of Fruit and Vegetable Products** is a systematic, scientific evaluation procedure that quantifies the physical, chemical, microbiological, and sensory attributes of processed or fresh horticultural produce against pre-established standards, regulatory limits, and consumer expectations. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme for the Food Analysis and Quality Evaluation Lab, this experiment integrates **objective instrumental measurements** (refractometry, titrimetry, spectrophotometry) with **subjective sensory evaluation** (9-point hedonic scale) to certify product acceptability, nutritional integrity, and shelf-life stability.

The term **Quality** in this context is a multi-dimensional construct encompassing:

- **Intrinsic Quality:** Compositional factors (moisture, sugars, acids, vitamins, pigments)
- **Extrinsic Quality:** Appearance, size, color uniformity, absence of defects
- **Hidden Quality:** Safety, microbiological load, pesticide residues, adulterants
- **Sensory Quality:** Taste, texture, aroma, mouthfeel as perceived by trained panelists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Module 2 explicitly requires students to perform hands-on quality analysis of *at least three fruit and vegetable products* (e.g., jam, squash, pickles, dried powders, beverages) using standard **BIS/IS/FSSAI/AOAC** methods. Every numerical report must be expressed with the correct **significant figures** and compared with the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** regulatory limits.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a packet of mango juice from a store. Before tasting it, your eyes register the **vibrant yellow color**, the label claims *"100% pure, no added sugar, Vitamin C enriched."* When you taste it, you perceive a balance of **sweetness, tanginess, and thickness** — these are the *sensory* parameters. Behind the scenes, a food analyst in a lab has already measured:

- The **°Brix** (sugar content) using a refractometer
- The **titratable acidity** by neutralizing with a standard base
- The **Vitamin C** by titrating against a dye (DCPIP/2,6-dichlorophenol indophenol)
- The **color intensity** using a spectrophotometer at 420 nm

Think of **Quality Analysis** as a *health check-up for food* — just as a doctor measures blood pressure, cholesterol, and sugar levels to certify a person's health, a food analyst measures moisture, acidity, and vitamins to certify a food's *health, safety, and acceptability*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Highlighted in KTU 2024 Curriculum:**
> - **Total Soluble Solids (TSS):** Expressed in **°Brix** — **1 °Brix = 1% w/w sucrose at 20 °C**
> - **Titratable Acidity (TA):** Expressed as **% citric acid** (for fruits) or **% acetic acid** (for pickles/fermented products)
> - **pH:** Dimensionless, measured on a **0–14 scale** using a calibrated digital pH meter
> - **Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C):** Expressed in **mg/100 g** of sample

### Key Standards & Regulatory Bodies Referenced in KTU Lab Manual

| Standard Body | Full Form | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **FSSAI** | Food Safety and Standards Authority of India | Mandatory quality limits in India |
| **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards (IS-series) | Indian product specifications |
| **AOAC** | Association of Official Analytical Chemists | Official analytical methods (international) |
| **Codex Alimentarius** | FAO/WHO Food Standards | Global reference for trade |
| **Agmark** | Agricultural Marketing (Govt. of India) | Voluntary grading for horticultural produce |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quality Analysis Workflow — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 lab module follows a **five-stage systematic workflow** for quality analysis. Each stage builds on the previous one, and skipping any stage leads to incomplete reporting (a common deduction in KTU practical exams).

**Stage 1 — Sample Preparation**
- Homogenization of the product (mortar-pestle, blender, or stomacher)
- Weighing of representative aliquots on an **analytical balance** (readability 0.0001 g)
- Filtration/centrifugation for clear extracts

**Stage 2 — Physicochemical Profiling**
- Measurement of **pH** (potentiometric method)
- Determination of **TSS** (refractometric method)
- Estimation of **titratable acidity** (acid-base titration)
- Calculation of **TSS/TA ratio** (the *Maturity Index* for fruits)

**Stage 3 — Nutritional Profiling**
- **Ascorbic acid** by visual titrimetry (DCPIP method) or HPLC
- **Reducing sugars** by Lane-Eynon or DNSA method
- **Total sugars** after acid hydrolysis of sucrose
- **Moisture/Ash** by gravimetric oven/muffle furnace methods

**Stage 4 — Instrumental Quality Assessment**
- **Color** analysis (L*, a*, b* CIE coordinates or absorbance at λ_max)
- **Texture** (penetration force, firmness using a penetrometer or Texture Analyzer)
- **Viscosity** (Brookfield viscometer for juices/sauces)

**Stage 5 — Sensory Evaluation**
- Panel of 10–15 semi-trained panelists
- **9-point Hedonic Scale** (1 = Dislike extremely, 9 = Like extremely)
- Scoring for appearance, color, flavor, taste, texture, overall acceptability
- Statistical analysis of scores (mean ± standard deviation)

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Critical Measurement

| Parameter | Why it Matters | How it is Measured | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture** | Predicts shelf life; high moisture → microbial spoilage | Hot air oven (105 °C, 3–5 h) to constant weight | High |
| **TSS (°Brix)** | Indicator of sweetness, maturity, and fruit-juice authenticity | Abbe/Digital refractometer at 20 °C | Very High |
| **Titratable Acidity** | Affects flavor balance; legal limit for many products | Titration with 0.1 N NaOH using phenolphthalein | Very High |
| **pH** | Determines microbial safety (low pH inhibits pathogens) | Calibrated digital pH meter (buffer pH 4, 7, 9.2) | High |
| **Ascorbic Acid** | Nutritional quality; antioxidant indicator | DCPIP titration (decolorization endpoint) | Very High |
| **Sugars** | Sweetness, calorie content, fermentation control | Lane-Eynon / DNSA / Anthrone method | Medium |
| **Color** | Consumer acceptance, processing adequacy | Spectrophotometer / Colorimeter (L*a*b*) | Medium |
| **Sensory** | Ultimate test of consumer acceptability | Hedonic scaling by trained panel | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **TSS/TA ratio** is the *single most favorite derived parameter* in KTU exams. A ratio of **15–20** indicates a balanced, ripe fruit; below **10** indicates excessive sourness; above **30** indicates insipid sweetness. Always state this ratio with two significant figures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Mathematical Toolkit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and form the **core numerical toolkit** expected in KTU lab viva and record submissions. Master these — they appear in **Part A (3-mark)** and **Part B (14-mark)** questions every semester.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units & Constants | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moisture Content (% w/w)** | $MC = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$ | W₀ = empty dish (g); W₁ = dish + sample before drying (g); W₂ = dish + sample after drying (g) | AOAC 934.01 |
| 2 | **Total Soluble Solids (°Brix)** | Direct reading on refractometer, corrected to 20 °C | 1 °Brix ≡ 1% sucrose w/w | AOAC 932.12 |
| 3 | **Titratable Acidity (% citric acid)** | $TA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 0.064 \times 100}{W}$ | V = titre volume (mL); N = NaOH normality; 0.064 = meq. weight of citric acid (g); W = sample weight (g) | AOAC 942.15 |
| 4 | **Titratable Acidity (% acetic acid, for pickles)** | $TA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 0.060 \times 100}{W}$ | 0.060 = meq. weight of acetic acid (g) | AOAC 920.123 |
| 5 | **Ascorbic Acid (mg/100 g)** | $AA = \dfrac{V \times D \times 100}{W \times 1000} \times 100 = \dfrac{V \times D \times 10}{W}$ | V = titre (mL dye); D = dye factor (mg ascorbic acid/mL); W = sample (g) | AOAC 967.21 |
| 6 | **TSS/TA Ratio (Maturity Index)** | $Ratio = \dfrac{\text{°Brix}}{\% \text{ Acid}}$ | Dimensionless; ideal = 15–20 | Ranganna, 2017 |
| 7 | **Reducing Sugars (%) — Lane-Eynon** | $RS = \dfrac{25 \times F \times D}{T \times W} \times 100$ | F = Fehling's factor; D = dilution; T = titre (mL); W = sample (g) | AOAC 906.01 |
| 8 | **Total Sugars (%)** | $TS = RS + \dfrac{\text{Sucrose} \times 0.95}{1}$ | 0.95 = stoichiometric conversion factor (162/171, sucrose → invert sugar) | Standard reference |
| 9 | **Color Difference ΔE (CIE L\*a\*b\*)** | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | Dimensionless; ΔE > 2 = perceptible to human eye | CIE 1976 |
| 10 | **Sensory Score (Mean ± SD)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ ; $SD = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | n = number of panelists (typically 10–15) | Standard sensory method |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE SYMBOL SAFEGUARD:** Note that in the table above, all **absolute value** and **magnitude** references are written as plain words (e.g., "titre volume (mL)") rather than using the vertical pipe symbol **\|**, which would break the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

The methodologies mastered in this experiment are not confined to academic labs — they are the **backbone of the food processing industry**:

1. **Beverage Industry (e.g., Tropicana, Dabur, ITC Foods):** Brix-acid ratio is the single most critical control parameter ensuring batch-to-batch consistency of fruit juices.
2. **Dried Products Industry (e.g., MTR, Haldiram's):** Moisture content below **6–8%** is mandated for shelf-stable powders; failure leads to caking and mold growth.
3. **Pickle & Fermented Products:** Total acidity (as acetic acid) must be ≥ **1.0%** w/w for FSSAI compliance in table olives and ≥ **0.6%** for pickled cucumbers to prevent *Clostridium botulinum* growth.
4. **Export Houses:** Ascorbic acid and color (L\*a\*b\*) measurements are used to certify that frozen mango pulp meets **APEDA (Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority)** export quality norms.
5. **Quality Control Labs:** Sensory panels integrated with instrumental analysis form the foundation of **HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)** plans.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of the Three Most Critical KTU Exam Formulas

The following derivations are presented with **exhaustive step-by-step algebraic transitions**. Every logical step is explained in words. These three formulas are the **most frequently asked derivation questions** in the KTU 2024 lab record viva (worth up to 3 marks each in Part A and 7 marks in Part B).

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Moisture Content Formula

**Starting Principle:** Moisture is removed from a pre-weighed food sample by evaporation in a hot-air oven at 100–105 °C until a **constant weight** is achieved. The mass loss equals the moisture originally present in the sample.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**

Let:
- $W_0$ = mass of the empty, dry, clean moisture dish (g)
- $W_1$ = mass of the dish + wet sample (before drying) (g)
- $W_2$ = mass of the dish + dried sample (after oven drying to constant weight) (g)

**Step 2 — Express the mass of water lost:**

$$
m_{water} = W_1 - W_2
$$

*Explanation:* The dish is common to both weighings, so subtracting $W_2$ from $W_1$ cancels out the dish mass and leaves only the water that evaporated.

**Step 3 — Express the mass of the original (wet) sample:**

$$
m_{sample} = W_1 - W_0
$$

*Explanation:* We subtract the empty dish mass from the dish-plus-wet-sample mass to obtain the mass of the food sample before drying.

**Step 4 — Form the mass fraction of water:**

By definition, the mass fraction of moisture is:

$$
\text{Mass fraction of moisture} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{sample}} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage:**

Multiply the dimensionless fraction by 100 to express moisture as a percentage (w/w):

$$
\boxed{MC\,(\%\,w/w) = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The constant $W_0$ is often substituted in viva to test whether the student understands the role of the empty-dish mass. Always state $W_0$ explicitly. If a *pre-weighed aluminium dish* is used (as in some labs), then $W_0$ is taken from the manufacturer's certificate and quoted with the correct number of significant figures.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Titratable Acidity Formula (Expressed as % Citric Acid)

**Starting Principle:** The organic acids in a fruit/vegetable product (predominantly citric acid in citrus and most fruits; malic acid in apples; tartaric acid in grapes) are neutralized by a standard alkali (NaOH) of known normality. The volume of alkali consumed is stoichiometrically equivalent to the moles of acid present.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced neutralization reaction (for citric acid, a triprotic acid):**

$$
C_6H_8O_7 + 3\,NaOH \rightarrow Na_3C_6H_5O_7 + 3\,H_2O
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the equivalence relation $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$:**

The milli-equivalents (meq) of acid = milli-equivalents of NaOH used.

$$
\text{meq of acid in aliquot} = N \times V
$$

where:
- $N$ = normality of NaOH (typically 0.1 N)
- $V$ = volume of NaOH run down from the burette (mL)

**Step 3 — Convert meq of acid to mass of acid (in grams):**

By definition, $\text{meq} = \dfrac{\text{mass (mg)}}{\text{equivalent weight}}$. Rearranging:

$$
\text{Mass of acid (g)} = \dfrac{N \times V \times E}{1000}
$$

where $E$ is the equivalent weight of the dominant acid. For **citric acid**:
- Molecular weight of $C_6H_8O_7$ = **192.12 g/mol**
- Basicity (n) = 3 (three replaceable $H^+$)
- Equivalent weight $E = \dfrac{192.12}{3} = 64.04$ ≈ **0.064 g/meq** (the constant used in KTU labs)

**Step 4 — Express acidity as percentage of the weighed sample:**

The aliquot titrated is part of a known sample weight $W$ (in grams). So:

$$
\%\,Acidity = \dfrac{\text{mass of acid (g)}}{\text{mass of sample (g)}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the mass of acid from Step 3:

$$
\%\,Acidity = \dfrac{N \times V \times E \times 100}{1000 \times W}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using $E = 0.064$ g/meq for citric acid:**

$$
\boxed{TA\,(\%\,citric\,acid) = \dfrac{V \times N \times 0.064 \times 100}{W}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Rule for Other Acid Systems:**
> - For **acetic acid** (pickles, vinegar): use $E = 0.060$ g/meq (MW = 60.05, n = 1)
> - For **malic acid** (apples, pears): use $E = 0.067$ g/meq (MW = 134.09, n = 2)
> - For **lactic acid** (fermented vegetables, sauerkraut): use $E = 0.090$ g/meq (MW = 90.08, n = 1)
> - For **tartaric acid** (grapes, tamarind): use $E = 0.075$ g/meq (MW = 150.09, n = 2)
>
> **KTU Viva Tip:** Always ask your examiner which acid system they want you to assume, and explicitly state your choice in the answer.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Ascorbic Acid (DCPIP) Formula

**Starting Principle:** 2,6-Dichlorophenol Indophenol (DCPIP) is a redox dye that is **blue in its oxidized form** and **colorless when reduced**. Ascorbic acid reduces DCPIP quantitatively, and the endpoint is reached when a faint pink color persists for **5–10 seconds**.

The stoichiometry of the reaction is:

$$
\text{Ascorbic acid} + DCPIP_{(blue)} \rightarrow \text{Dehydroascorbic acid} + DCPIP_{(colorless)}
$$

**Step 1 — Standardize the DCPIP dye:**

The dye factor $D$ is determined by titrating a known mass of pure ascorbic acid (typically 100 mg dissolved in 100 mL of 3% metaphosphoric acid) against the dye until the endpoint.

$$
D = \dfrac{\text{mass of ascorbic acid (mg)}}{\text{volume of dye used (mL)}}
$$

Units of D: **mg of ascorbic acid equivalent per mL of dye.**

**Step 2 — Titrate the sample extract:**

A known weight $W$ of the sample is extracted with metaphosphoric acid (which stabilizes ascorbic acid by chelating metal ions and inhibiting oxidation by ascorbic acid oxidase), made up to a known volume $V_{total}$, and an aliquot $V_{aliquot}$ is titrated against the standardized DCPIP. Let the titre be $T$ mL.

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid in the titrated aliquot:**

$$
\text{Mass of ascorbic acid in aliquot} = T \times D \text{ (mg)}
$$

**Step 4 — Scale up to the total extract volume:**

$$
\text{Mass of ascorbic acid in total extract} = T \times D \times \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{aliquot}} \text{ (mg)}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as mg per 100 g of sample:**

$$
\text{Ascorbic acid} = \dfrac{T \times D \times V_{total} \times 100}{V_{aliquot} \times W \times 1000} \text{ (mg/100 g)}
$$

The factor $\frac{100}{1000} = \frac{1}{10}$ converts mg → g then per 100 g basis.

**Step 6 — Simplified KTU Final Formula (when $V_{total} = 100$ mL, $V_{aliquot} = 10$ mL, and the dilution factor is 10):**

$$
\boxed{AA\,(mg/100\,g) = \dfrac{T \times D \times 10}{W}}
$$

*Verification:* If $T = 5$ mL, $D = 0.5$ mg/mL, $W = 2$ g, then $AA = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 10}{2} = 12.5$ mg/100 g. ✓ This corresponds to a typical value for a moderately rich fruit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in DCPIP Titration (deduct 1–2 marks each in KTU viva):**
> 1. Failing to mention **3% metaphosphoric acid (HPO₃)** as the extractant — it is **NOT** distilled water.
> 2. Confusing the **dye factor** (mg/mL) with the **normality** of the dye.
> 3. Not standardizing the dye on the **same day** of the experiment (DCPIP deteriorates with light exposure).
> 4. Missing the **endpoint description** (faint pink that persists for 5–10 s).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Calculation)

**Question (KTU Pattern):** A fruit squash sample (5.0 g) was titrated against 0.1 N NaOH, and the titre value was 4.2 mL. Calculate the titratable acidity as % citric acid. The °Brix of the same sample was 18.5. Comment on the Brix-Acid ratio and shelf-life quality.

**Step 1 — Apply the TA formula:**

$$
TA = \dfrac{V \times N \times 0.064 \times 100}{W}
$$

$$
TA = \dfrac{4.2 \times 0.1 \times 0.064 \times 100}{5.0}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute step-by-step:**

- Numerator: $4.2 \times 0.1 = 0.42$
- Numerator: $0.42 \times 0.064 = 0.02688$
- Numerator: $0.02688 \times 100 = 2.688$
- Denominator: $5.0$
- Division: $2.688 / 5.0 = 0.5376$

$$
\boxed{TA = 0.54\% \text{ citric acid (rounded to 2 significant figures)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Brix/Acid Ratio:**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{18.5}{0.54} = 34.26 \approx 34.3
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A ratio of 34.3 is **significantly higher than the ideal 15–20 range**, indicating that the squash is *overly sweet and under-acid*. For commercial squashes, the recommended ratio is **30–35** (since sugar is added during squashes manufacture), so the value is **acceptable for a sweetened squash** but **not for a fresh juice**. To improve the product, the manufacturer could increase citric acid content slightly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Standard:** Always quote answers to **two significant figures** for TA and three significant figures for °Brix. Ratios should be expressed to **one decimal place**. All units must be in **SI base** form.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Quality Analysis Workflow

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **interactions between analytical modules** in a KTU-style quality analysis experiment. The block-level architecture clearly shows how a single sample is split into multiple parallel analysis streams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Receipt<br>and Registration] --> B[Sample<br>Homogenization]
    B --> C1[Moisture<br>Determination]
    B --> C2[pH<br>Measurement]
    B --> C3[TSS by<br>Refractometer]
    B --> C4[Acid-Base<br>Titration]
    B --> C5[DCPIP<br>Titration]
    B --> C6[Color Analysis<br>Spectrophotometer]
    B --> C7[Sensory<br>Evaluation]
    C1 --> D[Data Compilation<br>and Statistical Analysis]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    D --> E{Compliance Check<br>vs FSSAI Limits}
    E --> F1[Acceptable]
    E --> F2[Rejected]
    F1 --> G[Final Quality<br>Report]
    F2 --> H[Process<br>Re-engineering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each node (C1–C7) represents an **independent analytical stream** operating on a sub-sample of the homogenized product (B). All streams converge at the data compilation node (D), where the mean, standard deviation, and Brix-Acid ratio are calculated. The decision diamond (E) performs a binary compliance check against FSSAI or Agmark standards, branching the workflow into **Acceptance** or **Rejection** paths.

### 4.2 Schematic — Sequential Titrations in Acid-Base Workflow

This second Mermaid diagram depicts the **titrative procedure** for the most common KTU lab titration, the **Titratable Acidity Determination**. It emphasizes the *stepwise decision logic* in reaching the phenolphthalein endpoint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Weigh 5-10 g<br>of homogenized sample] --> S1[Transfer to 100 mL<br>volumetric flask]
    S1 --> S2[Make up to volume<br>with distilled water]
    S2 --> S3[Filter through<br>Whatman No. 1]
    S3 --> S4[Pipette 10-25 mL<br>aliquot into conical flask]
    S4 --> S5[Add 2-3 drops of<br>phenolphthalein indicator]
    S5 --> S6[Titrate against 0.1 N<br>NaOH from burette]
    S6 --> Q1{Persistent pink<br>color for 15 sec?}
    Q1 -->|No| S6
    Q1 -->|Yes| S7[Note burette<br>reading V mL]
    S7 --> S8[Repeat in triplicate<br>and take mean]
    S8 --> S9[Calculate % acidity<br>using formula]
    S9 --> S10[Report with units<br>and significant figures]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Sensory Evaluation Panel Protocol

The third diagram maps the **9-point Hedonic Scale workflow** used in sensory evaluation, as required by KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Recruit 10-15<br>Panelists] --> P1[Screening<br>by Triangle Test]
    P1 --> P2[Training Session<br>3-5 sessions of 1 hr]
    P2 --> P3[Serve Sample<br>at 20-25 C]
    P3 --> P4[Panelist Rates on<br>9-point Hedonic Scale]
    P4 --> P5[Attributes: Color<br>Flavor, Texture, Aroma]
    P5 --> P6[Collect Score<br>Sheets]
    P6 --> P7[Calculate Mean<br>and Standard Deviation]
    P7 --> P8[Acceptability if<br>Mean Score >= 6]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Manual Compliance:** The 9-point Hedonic Scale anchors are: **9 = Like extremely, 8 = Like very much, 7 = Like moderately, 6 = Like slightly, 5 = Neither like nor dislike, 4 = Dislike slightly, 3 = Dislike moderately, 2 = Dislike very much, 1 = Dislike extremely.** A product with a mean score ≥ 6 is considered *acceptable*; ≥ 7 is *well-accepted* in commercial sensory trials.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the term **Brix-Acid Ratio**. What is its significance in evaluating the quality of fruit juices and squashes? State the ideal range for a balanced fruit juice.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Brix-Acid Ratio (also called the **Maturity Index** or **Sugar-Acid Ratio**) is the **dimensionless ratio** of the Total Soluble Solids (expressed in °Brix) to the Titratable Acidity (expressed as % dominant acid) of a fruit product.

$$
\text{Brix-Acid Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{°Brix}}{\%\,Acidity}
$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. It is a **single numerical indicator** of the balance between sweetness and sourness in a fruit product.
2. For **fresh fruit juices**, the ideal ratio is **15–20** — this range corresponds to a pleasant, harmonious taste.
3. For **sweetened squashes and cordials** (which have added sugar), the acceptable ratio is higher, typically **30–35**.
4. It is used as a **maturity indicator** in raw fruit grading — fruits harvested at the right stage have a ratio of 15–20.

**Ideal Range (1.5 marks):**
- Fresh fruit juice: **15–20**
- Sweetened squash: **30–35**
- Citrus juices (orange, lemon): 10–15 (more acidic)
- Mango pulp: 20–25

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is **3% metaphosphoric acid** used as the extractant for ascorbic acid determination, instead of distilled water? Mention the principle of the DCPIP titration method.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Use of Metaphosphoric Acid (1.5 marks):**
Metaphosphoric acid (HPO₃) is preferred over distilled water for ascorbic acid extraction because:
1. It **chelates metal ions** (especially Cu²⁺ and Fe²⁺) that catalyse the oxidation of ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid in the presence of atmospheric oxygen.
2. It creates a **strongly acidic environment (pH < 2)** that inhibits the enzyme **ascorbic acid oxidase**, which is naturally present in fruits and vegetables and rapidly degrades Vitamin C.
3. It **precipitates proteins** in the extract, giving a clearer filtrate for accurate titration.

**Principle of DCPIP Titration (1.5 marks):**
2,6-Dichlorophenol Indophenol (DCPIP) is a redox indicator dye. The **oxidized (blue)** form of DCPIP is reduced to a **colorless (leuco)** form by ascorbic acid. The titration endpoint is reached when all the ascorbic acid is consumed and the **faint pink color of the unreduced dye persists for 5–10 seconds**. The volume of dye consumed is stoichiometrically proportional to the ascorbic acid concentration in the sample.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two sub-options. The marking scheme is uniform: **Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks.** You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Set 2]**

**(a) Describe the procedure for the determination of Titratable Acidity of a fruit juice sample. Calculate the % acidity of a sample (expressed as citric acid) if 10 mL of juice (density 1.04 g/mL) required 6.8 mL of 0.1 N NaOH for neutralization. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Sample preparation:** Take 10 mL of the well-mixed fruit juice and transfer it to a 100 mL volumetric flask. Make up the volume to 100 mL with freshly boiled and cooled distilled water. This is the **working extract**.
2. **Filtration:** Filter the diluted extract through Whatman No. 1 filter paper to obtain a clear solution, free from pulp and suspended particles.
3. **Aliquot titration:** Pipette **25 mL** of the clear filtrate into a clean 250 mL conical (Erlenmeyer) flask.
4. **Indicator addition:** Add 2–3 drops of **1% phenolphthalein indicator** solution. The solution remains colorless at this stage.
5. **Titration:** Titrate the contents against **standardized 0.1 N NaOH** from a clean burette. Add NaOH in small increments (0.5 mL) with constant swirling. As the endpoint approaches, the pink color appears locally and fades; add NaOH **dropwise** near the endpoint.
6. **Endpoint:** Stop titration when a **persistent faint pink color** develops and remains for **at least 15 seconds**.
7. **Record the burette reading** as the titre value. Repeat the titration in **triplicate** for statistical reliability. The mean titre (within ±0.1 mL agreement) is used for calculation. A blank titration is also run with 25 mL distilled water + indicator.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

- Volume of juice taken, $V_{sample} = 10$ mL
- Density, $\rho = 1.04$ g/mL
- Mass of juice, $W = 10 \times 1.04 = 10.4$ g
- Titre, $V = 6.8$ mL
- Normality of NaOH, $N = 0.1$ N
- Equivalent weight of citric acid, $E = 0.064$ g/meq

**Apply the formula:**

$$
TA\,(\%\,citric\,acid) = \dfrac{V \times N \times E \times 100}{W}
$$

$$
TA = \dfrac{6.8 \times 0.1 \times 0.064 \times 100}{10.4}
$$

**Step-wise calculation:**

- Numerator: $6.8 \times 0.1 = 0.68$
- Numerator: $0.68 \times 0.064 = 0.04352$
- Numerator: $0.04352 \times 100 = 4.352$
- Denominator: $10.4$
- Division: $4.352 / 10.4 = 0.4184...$

$$
\boxed{TA = 0.42\% \text{ citric acid (w/w)}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The fruit juice contains **0.42% titratable acidity** as citric acid, which is typical for a mildly acidic fruit juice such as sweet orange or mango nectar.

---

**(b) Explain the **9-point Hedonic Scale** method of sensory evaluation. List the score anchors and discuss its advantages and limitations in the quality assessment of fruit and vegetable products. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The 9-point Hedonic Scale, developed by **Peryam and Pilgrim (1957)**, is the most widely used affective sensory test in the food industry. It quantifies the **degree of liking** or disliking of a product on a balanced 9-point verbal scale ranging from "dislike extremely" to "like extremely."

**Score Anchors (1 mark):**

| Score | Verbal Anchor |
|---|---|
| 9 | Like extremely |
| 8 | Like very much |
| 7 | Like moderately |
| 6 | Like slightly |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike |
| 4 | Dislike slightly |
| 3 | Dislike moderately |
| 2 | Dislike very much |
| 1 | Dislike extremely |

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. A semi-trained panel of **10 to 15 panelists** is recruited, screened (by triangle or duo-trio test for sensory acuity), and trained in 3–5 sessions of 1 hour each.
2. Samples are served in **random order** at a controlled temperature (20–25 °C) in **identical, coded containers** under white light to eliminate bias.
3. Panelists rate the sample on the 9-point scale for attributes: **appearance, color, aroma, taste, texture, and overall acceptability.**
4. Scores are tabulated, and the **mean ± standard deviation** is calculated.
5. A product is considered **acceptable** if the mean score is **≥ 6**.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple to administer; minimal training required.
- Provides a **quantitative measure** of consumer liking that can be statistically analyzed.
- Direct reflection of **consumer perception** (the ultimate test of quality).
- Useful for **product development** and **reformulation** (e.g., reducing sugar, increasing flavor).

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Subjective** — influenced by individual panelist preferences, mood, and culture.
- **Hedonic bias** — panelists may rate a product higher than they actually feel to please the experimenter (the "halo effect").
- Not suitable for **discrimination testing** (use triangle test instead).
- A single score does not capture **which attribute** drives liking or disliking.
- Fatigue and carry-over effects between samples can distort results if not controlled.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a) Describe the DCPIP titrimetric method for the determination of ascorbic acid in a fruit juice sample. A 20 mL sample of orange juice (density 1.03 g/mL) was diluted to 100 mL with 3% HPO₃. A 10 mL aliquot required 8.4 mL of DCPIP dye (factor = 0.5 mg/mL) for the endpoint. Calculate the ascorbic acid content in mg/100 mL of juice. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reagents Required (1 mark):**
1. **3% Metaphosphoric acid (HPO₃)** — extraction and stabilizing medium.
2. **Standard DCPIP dye solution** (0.25 mM) standardized daily.
3. **Standard ascorbic acid** for dye standardization.

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Standardization of DCPIP dye:** Dissolve 100 mg of pure L-ascorbic acid in 100 mL of 3% HPO₃. Pipette 5 mL (5 mg ascorbic acid) into a conical flask and titrate against the DCPIP dye from a burette until a **faint pink color persists for 5 seconds**. Note the titre and calculate the dye factor:

$$
D = \dfrac{\text{Mass of ascorbic acid (mg)}}{\text{Volume of dye (mL)}}
$$

2. **Sample extraction:** Accurately weigh or pipette 20 mL of well-mixed orange juice (density 1.03 g/mL) into a 100 mL volumetric flask. Add 5 mL of 3% HPO₃, swirl, and make up to 100 mL with **the same acid**. Mix thoroughly and filter through Whatman No. 1.
3. **Titration:** Pipette 10 mL of the clear filtrate into a 100 mL conical flask. Add 1 mL of the stabilizing acid. Titrate against the standardized DCPIP dye until a **faint pink color persists for at least 5 seconds**. Note the titre. Repeat in triplicate.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

- Sample volume, $V_{sample} = 20$ mL
- Density of juice, $\rho = 1.03$ g/mL
- Final extract volume, $V_{total} = 100$ mL
- Aliquot titrated, $V_{aliquot} = 10$ mL
- Titre, $T = 8.4$ mL
- Dye factor, $D = 0.5$ mg/mL

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
AA\,(mg/100\,mL) = \dfrac{T \times D \times V_{total} \times 100}{V_{aliquot} \times V_{sample}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
AA = \dfrac{8.4 \times 0.5 \times 100 \times 100}{10 \times 20}
$$

**Step 4 — Step-by-step evaluation:**

- Numerator (left): $8.4 \times 0.5 = 4.2$
- Numerator: $4.2 \times 100 = 420$
- Numerator: $420 \times 100 = 42{,}000$
- Denominator: $10 \times 20 = 200$
- Division: $42{,}000 / 200 = 210$

$$
\boxed{AA = 210 \text{ mg/100 mL of orange juice}}
$$

**Comment (optional, 1 mark):** This value is **atypically high** (typical orange juice contains 30–50 mg/100 mL), suggesting either a fortified product or an experimental error in dilution. The student is expected to compare with the **FSSAI limit** of ≥ 20 mg/100 g for "fruit juice" labelled products.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–1 mark):** Students forget to include the factor of **100** in the numerator (to convert per-aliquot to per-100-mL basis) and the factor of **1000** in the denominator (to convert mg → g if working in g/100 mL). The simplified KTU formula $AA = \frac{T \times D \times 10}{W}$ only applies when $V_{total} = 100$ mL, $V_{aliquot} = 10$ mL, AND $W$ is in grams. **Always derive the formula from first principles in the answer.**
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Not mentioning the **3% metaphosphoric acid** as the extractant — failure to do so is a 1-mark deduction in KTU viva.
